tipRules.tip: Johnston: In cold water around 40 degrees, slow-twitch a suspending jerkbait for largemouth bass that are suspending in the water column. Target the middle to bottom of the water column and work lures with a sluggish style.
Johnston: Use a 3/8 to 3/4-ounce football jig in natural coloring like green pumpkin and bounce it off the bottom for cold-water largemouth that are sitting on or close to the bottom.

Johnston: In cold-water months, slow drag jigs. Use drift paddles to dial down drift speed and control your presentation for more effective casting and a better hookup ratio. Be methodical to stay in the strike zone.

knowledge.insight: Johnston: Everything from your technique to the fish themselves pretty much slows down when it gets cold in that 40-degree range. You have to adapt and change tactics — target the middle to bottom of the water column and work lures with a sluggish style.
Johnston: For smallmouth bass in cold water, they seem to key in on tube jigs or a swimbait better than jerkbaits. For largemouth, suspending jerkbaits and deep-drop jigs are the go-to.

Johnston: In cold water when fishing shallower 2- to 10-foot depths, anchor outside of a sand patch, boulder, or weed edge and work the area hard for multiple hookups. These bass are moving super slow — you really have to work an area over hard with repeated casts to get a strike.
